text:
Academic Advising
Purpose for New StudentsHelps new students smoothly adapt to university study life. Provides guidance on major selection, effective study skills development and phased study plan formulation. Introduces diverse extended learning opportunities including exchange, internship, experiential learning, service-learning and residential education beyond formal curriculum.
Continued Support after First YearOffers ongoing guidance for students to update study plans, connect academic learning with post-graduation development, and set clear career goals or further study plans.
Expected Student OutcomesWith academic advising and active participation, students can:
Adapt to the university learning environment efficiently
Select suitable study strategies, majors, concentrations, minors and courses for personal growth and career development
Develop comprehensive study plans and monitor academic progress throughout university
Formulate preparation plans for future careers or postgraduate studies
Utilize university resources to optimize academic performance
Mandatory Advisor Meeting Requirements
First-year students: At least one meeting with academic advisor per semester
Year 2 and above students: Voluntary meetings as needed for academic progress consultation
Mandatory meetings in any academic year under the following circumstances: Declare/change a major; After advisor replacement; Placed on academic probation; Opt out of Transdisciplinary Second Major (TSM)/Second Major (SM)
Types of Academic Advisors & Core Responsibilities
Faculty/Programme Academic Advising Coordinator: Overall coordination of all advising activities, supervise student academic development
Faculty/Programme Academic Advisors: Assist freshmen adaptation, major selection and general study planning; support transdisciplinary curriculum consultation
Department Academic Advisors: Guide major course selection, professional study planning, career transition for Year 1 (programme admission) and Year 2+ students
TSM/SM Academic Staff: Provide TSM/SM programme details and professional course selection advice
Designated Advisors for Individualized Majors: Assist adaptation, major/course selection and personalized study plan formulation
Access to Full GuidelinesThe complete official academic advising guidelines are available on BUniPort.

# Academic Dress for Graduates
## 1. Gown
- Doctor (Honoris Causa): Dark red velvet robe, 4.5-inch gold satin front facings trimmed with middle blue satin
- DBA / EdD / PhD: Black robe with royal blue velvet facings and three royal blue velvet sleeve strips
- MPhil / Taught Master: Black robe of Oxford MA pattern
- Postgraduate Diploma: Black robe of Oxford MA pattern
- Bachelor: Simple traditional style black robe

## 2. Hood
- Doctor (Honoris Causa): Dark red velvet hood lined with gold satin
- DBA: Black hood with full blue & gold silk lining, silver grey edge lining + 1cm parallel same-color extra lining
- EdD: Black hood with full blue & gold silk lining, citron edge lining + 1cm parallel same-color extra lining
- PhD: Black hood with full blue & gold silk lining, royal blue velvet edge lining
- MPhil: Black hood with full blue & gold silk lining, deep purple edge lining
- Taught Master: Black hood with full blue & gold silk lining, degree-specific edge lining + 1cm parallel same-degree-color extra lining
- Bachelor: Black hood with full blue & gold silk lining, degree-specific edge lining

## 3. Cap
- Doctor (Honoris Causa): Black velvet bonnet with gold tassel
- DBA: Black bonnet cap with gold tassel
- EdD / PhD: Mortar-board cap with gold tassel
- MPhil / Taught Master / Postgraduate Diploma / Bachelor: Mortar-board cap with black tassel

## 4. Stole
- Postgraduate Diploma: Stole in discipline-specific colour

# Academic Integrity
Introduction
HKBU aims at developing all aspects of the Whole Person through education. Students are expected to excel academically and become responsible global citizens. The first Graduate Attribute - Citizenship, cultivates students to be responsible citizens with international outlook, ethics and civility. High academic integrity standards are required for all staff and students.

Academic integrity upholds six core fundamental values adapted from the International Center for Academic Integrity (2021):
1. Honesty: Be truthful about borrowed work, ideas and opinions
2. Trust: Build a respectful and fair campus community
3. Fairness: Complete independent work without plagiarizing others' ideas
4. Respect: Value diverse opinions and cite borrowed sources properly
5. Responsibility: Abide by integrity standards and take accountability for personal work
6. Courage: Resist plagiarism and academic cheating temptations

Definition of Academic Misconduct
All actions violating academic integrity, including exam rule breaches, cheating, plagiarism, impersonation, purchasing exam materials, data falsification and dishonest acts for academic credit (National Academic Integrity Network, 2021).

HKBU attaches great importance to academic integrity for students' holistic development and future careers; all students must maintain academic integrity at all times.

Consequences of Academic Dishonesty
Penalties include reduced grades, failing grades, study suspension or permanent academic dismissal.

Students' Core Responsibilities
1. Understand academic integrity rules and avoid dishonest behaviors strictly
2. Submit original independent work and present research findings honestly
3. Cite and reference all external sources correctly
4. Consult instructors for clarification when encountering doubts

Specific Types of Serious Academic Misconduct
1. Collusion: Jointly completing assignments for individual submission; lending work to others; allowing others to copy work
2. Ghost writing: Having others complete partial or full assignments on behalf of oneself
3. Essay and paper mills: Purchasing or downloading ready-made/machine-generated assignments from online platforms
4. Research fraud: Intentionally fabricating, falsifying or misrepresenting research data/methods

Note
Refer to official exam regulations for detailed handling rules and penalties for exam breaches and cheating incidents; the rules also apply to research postgraduate taught courses.

# Master of Science in Data Analytics and Artificial Intelligence
Programme Director: Prof LEUNG, Yiu Wing

Programme Overview
The programme focuses on the concepts and practices of information systems and covers three core themes: Data Analytics, Artificial Intelligence and Intelligent Information Systems. Students acquire theoretical knowledge and hands-on practical skills to solve real-world information system problems.

Upon completion, students will achieve the following learning outcomes:
1. Master the core concepts and methodologies of data analytics, artificial intelligence and intelligent information systems.
2. Apply relevant technologies to conduct problem-solving and practical applications.
3. Identify business needs and transform them into feasible information systems via data and AI technologies.

Study Mode & Duration
1. One-year Full-time: Normal 1 year, maximum 2 years (with approved extension)
2. Two-year Part-time: Normal 2 years, maximum 3 years (with approved extension)

Total Curriculum Structure
I Required Courses: 14 units
II Elective Courses: 15 units
Total Credit: 29 units

Detailed Requirements
I Required Courses (14 units)
COMP7015 Artificial Intelligence - 3 units
COMP7035 Python for Data Analytics and Artificial Intelligence - 3 units
COMP7180 Quantitative Methods for Data Analytics and Artificial Intelligence - 3 units
COMP7530 IT Forum - 1 unit
COMP7950 IT Project Skills - 1 unit
COMP7990 Principles and Practices of Data Analytics - 3 units

II Elective Courses (15 units)
Electives are divided into 4 categories:

1. Data Analytics Stream
COMP7170 Data Security and Privacy - 3 units
COMP7260 Special Topics in Data Analytics - 3 units
COMP7640 Database Systems and Administration - 3 units
COMP7650 Data Mining and Knowledge Discovery - 3 units
COMP7810 Business Intelligence - 3 units
COMP7930 Big Data Analytics - 3 units

2. Artificial Intelligence Stream
COMP7045 Natural Language Processing and Large Language Models - 3 units
COMP7055 Computer Vision - 3 units
COMP7125 Prompt Engineering for Generative AI - 3 units
COMP7220 Special Topics in Artificial Intelligence - 3 units
COMP7240 Recommender Systems - 3 units
COMP7250 Machine Learning - 3 units
COMP7630 Web Intelligence and Its Applications - 3 units

# Campus Locations & Transportation
## Kowloon Tong Campus
Comprises 4 sub-campuses: Baptist University Road Campus (BURC), Ho Sin Hang Campus (HSHC), Jockey Club Campus of Creativity (JC3), Shaw Campus (SHAW)

### Transportation
1. MTR
Nearest Station: Kowloon Tong Station
- Walking (10-15 mins): Exit A2 → Pass Suffolk Road → Waterloo Road (Northbound) → Right into Junction Road; HSHC on left, BURC/JC3/SHAW on right
- Minibus: Exit B/D to green minibus terminal; 29A for HSHC/SHAW; 25M/25MS for BURC/JC3

2. Bus Stops & Routes
Kam Shing Road: 2B, 2F, 3C, 7, 85, 85A, 85B, E22
Hong Kong Baptist University: 2B, 2F, 3C, 7, 85, 85A, 85B, 103, 208, E22
Renfrew Road: 103, 208

## Kai Tak Campus
Houses the Academy of Visual Arts; converted from Grade I historic former RAF Officers’ Mess with colonial architectural style

### Transportation
MTR Kwun Tong Line → Choi Hung Station Exit A2 → Pass Tsuen Shek House → Opposite Lam Shek House & Wong Shek House

## Shek Mun Campus
Houses College of International Education & HKBU Affiliated Wong Kam Fai Secondary and Primary School

### Transportation
MTR Ma On Shan Line → Shek Mun Station Exit B (follow directional signs)

# Certification of Academic Assessment
## Testimonials
Issued by: Academic Registry (undergraduate & taught postgraduate programmes) / Graduate School (research postgraduate programmes). Purpose: Official proof of student status. Personal recommendation letters should be requested from familiar faculty or staff members.

## Transcripts
Content: Student status, completed courses, grades and GPAs. Official validity: Must bear the designated authorized signature and university official stamp. Fee: Charged for transcript issuance.

## Diplomas
Awarded to graduates who meet all graduation requirements and obtain Senate approval; serves as the official graduation certificate.

Title (Units): COMP7125 Prompt Engineering for Generative AI (3,2,1)

Course Aims:
This course introduces students to prompt engineering for generative AI systems. With the advancement of large language models (LLMs) and other generative AI technologies, the ability to design effective prompts has become a critical skill. The course aims to provide students with both theoretical understanding and practical skills in crafting prompts that effectively communicate with and control generative AI systems. Students will learn the principles that govern different types of generative AI models, develop techniques to optimize prompt design, and build applications that leverage these powerful systems. Throughout the course, emphasis will be placed on ethical considerations, evaluation methodologies, and the practical applications of prompt engineering in real-world scenarios.

Prerequisite:
COMP7015 Artificial Intelligence or COMP7025 Artificial Intelligence for Digital Transformation

Course Intended Learning Outcomes (CILOs):
Upon successful completion of this course, students should be able to:
No. Course Intended Learning Outcomes (CILOs)

Knowledge
1 Describe the architecture and functions of generative AI models and explain the fundamental principles of prompt engineering.
2 Explain various prompt engineering techniques and their applications in different contexts.

Professional Skill
3 Design and optimize prompts for various generative AI applications, including content generation, information extraction, and decision support systems.
4 Implement prompt engineering techniques like few-shot prompting, chain-of-thought reasoning, and retrieval-augmented generation.

Transferable Skill
5 Evaluate the quality of generative AI responses and implement techniques to mitigate hallucinations and biases in outputs.

Attitude
6 Analyze the ethical considerations and limitations of prompt engineering in real-world applications.

Calendar Description:
This course introduces the principles and techniques of prompt engineering for generative AI models. Topics include AI model architecture, prompt design fundamentals, content retrieval and integration, prompt ensembling techniques, evaluation methods, and ethical considerations. Students will learn how to design effective prompts, build AI-powered applications, and develop conversational agents and complex workflows. The course emphasizes practical applications of concepts through case studies and hands-on assignments.

Teaching and Learning Activities (TLAs):
CILOs Type of TLA
1,2,5,6 Students will learn the fundamental principles and key concepts of prompt engineering via lectures.
3-6 Students will design and implement effective prompt engineering techniques

Learning Activities (TLAs):
CILOs Type of TLA
1,2,5,6 Students will learn the fundamental principles and key concepts of prompt engineering via lectures.
3-6 Students will design and implement effective prompt engineering techniques, evaluate response quality, and assess ethical considerations through tutorials, in-class exercises, and quizzes.
3-5 Students will work on written assignments to consolidate and apply what they have learned about prompt engineering and generative AI applications.

Assessment:
No. Assessment Methods Weighting CILOs to be addressed Description of Assessment Tasks
1 Tutorial and quizzes 20% 3-6 Tutorials and quizzes/in-class exercises are designed to evaluate students' understanding of prompt engineering concepts and their ability to apply these concepts to solve real-world problems.
2 Assignments 30% 3-5 Assignments will be used to assess students’ proficiency in designing and implementing effective prompts for problem-solving.
3 Examination 50% 1-3, 5-6 The final examination will comprehensively assess students' knowledge of prompt engineering concepts, ability to analyze prompt effectiveness, and understanding of ethical considerations in generative AI applications.

Assessment Rubrics:
Criteria Excellent (A) Good (B) Satisfactory (C) Marginal Pass (D) Fail (F)
Knowledge of prompt engineering concepts Demonstrates exceptional understanding of all prompt engineering concepts, principles, and advanced techniques Demonstrates strong understanding of most prompt engineering concepts with good accuracy Demonstrates adequate understanding of core prompt engineering concepts with minor misconceptions Demonstrates basic understanding of some prompt engineering concepts with limited accuracy Demonstrates minimal understanding of prompt engineering concepts, significant misconceptions
Prompt design skills Creates highly sophisticated prompts that address all requirements with elegant solutions Creates effective prompts that address most requirements with minor flaws Creates acceptable prompts that address some requirements with some limitations Creates basic prompts that partially address requirements with significant flaws Unable to create effective prompts, demonstrates fundamental misunderstanding of prompt design
Application of advanced techniques Consistently applies advanced techniques with exceptional results Frequently applies advanced techniques with good results Sometimes applies advanced techniques with acceptable results Rarely applies advanced techniques with poor results Unable to apply advanced techniques or applies them incorrectly
Evaluation skills Demonstrates exceptional ability to evaluate prompt effectiveness and AI responses Demonstrates strong ability to evaluate prompt effectiveness and AI responses Demonstrates adequate ability to evaluate prompt effectiveness and AI responses Demonstrates limited ability to evaluate prompt effectiveness and AI

valuation skills Demonstrates exceptional ability to evaluate prompt effectiveness and AI responses Demonstrates strong ability to evaluate prompt effectiveness and AI responses Demonstrates adequate ability to evaluate prompt effectiveness and AI responses Demonstrates limited ability to evaluate prompt effectiveness and AI responses Unable to evaluate prompt effectiveness or AI responses
Ethical awareness Demonstrates sophisticated awareness of ethical implications with nuanced analysis Demonstrates good awareness of ethical implications with clear reasoning Demonstrates basic awareness of ethical implications with some reasoning Demonstrates minimal awareness of ethical implications with superficial understanding Fails to recognize or address ethical implications of prompt engineering

Course Content and CILOs Mapping:
Content CILO No. Hours
I Introduction to Prompt Engineering and Generative AI Fundamentals 1,2,6 11
II Core Prompt Engineering Techniques 2-5 18
III Advanced Large Language Model (LLM) Topics 3-5 6
IV Evaluation, Ethics, and Future Directions 1,5,6 4

References:
- Berryman, J. & Ziegler, A. (2025). Prompt Engineering for LLMs: The Art and Science of Building Large Language Model-Based Applications. O'Reilly Media.
- Phoenix, J. & Taylor, M. (2024). Prompt Engineering for Generative AI: Future-Proof Inputs for Reliable AI Outputs. O'Reilly Media.
- Carnegie Mellon University. (2023). Prompt Engineering. [Course materials from CMU's Prompt Engineering course].
- Cognitive Class. (2023). Prompt Engineering for Generative AI. [IBM's Cognitive Class course materials].

Course Content:
I. Introduction to Prompt Engineering and Generative AI Fundamentals
A. Introduction to Prompt Engineering
B. Understanding Generative AI Models
C. Moving from Model Completion to Conversation

II. Core Prompt Engineering Techniques

C. Assembling Effective Prompts
D. Optimizing Model Responses

III. Advanced Large Language Model (LLM) Topics

B. LLM Workflows

IV. Evaluation, Ethics, and Future Directions
A. Evaluating LLM Applications
B. Ethical Considerations in Prompt Engineering
C. Multimodal Model

Applications

# Graduate Attributes
On graduation, HKBU Taught Postgraduate graduates should achieve the following six core attributes:

KNOWLEDGE
Master in-depth, advanced and up-to-date specialized knowledge; apply professional knowledge to practical disciplines or careers.

CREATIVITY
Make critical independent judgments via knowledge evaluation and application; provide creative and innovative solutions to practical problems.

SKILLS
Apply appropriate theories, methodologies and techniques to promote professional and disciplinary development.

LEARNING
Possess continuous learning motivation and ability; solve professional complexities through inquiry and critical reflection.

COMMUNICATION
Synthesize ideas clearly, coherently and logically with fluent expression and communication skills.

CITIZENSHIP
Abide by professional ethics and assume social responsibilities as local and global citizens.

Remark
HKBU whole person education covers seven elements. Teamwork is regarded as a pre-developed ability for postgraduates and not highlighted above. It may be set as learning goals in individual programmes based on curriculum characteristics.

Outcomes-Based Teaching and Learning
The ultimate aim of education at the University is to nurture students into whole persons; students are expected to attain all the 7 Graduate Attributes (GAs) on graduation and continue to develop these attributes as they progress in their lives. Towards this end, Outcomes-Based Teaching and Learning (OBTL) has been fully implemented by the University. OBTL focuses not on what the teachers intend to teach but rather on what the learners are able to do after their learning and graduation to the required standards.

At the course level, the Teaching and Learning Activities (TLAs) and the Assessment Methods (AMs) are all constructively aligned with the Course Intended Learning Outcomes (CILOs), i.e. Constructive Alignment. At the programme level, there are also specific sets of Programme Intended Learning Outcomes (PILOs), as the aim of having students complete all courses within a programme is to ensure that they have achieved the PILOs, which are ultimately aligned with the GAs.

To implement OBTL effectively, the University has fully adopted Criterion-Referenced Assessment (CRA) with the use of assessment rubrics to help assess how well students have achieved the learning outcomes based on objective criteria. Each student's grade is independent of any other students, and there is no pre-set distribution of grades for assessment.

OBTL,

use of assessment rubrics to help assess how well students have achieved the learning outcomes based on objective criteria. Each student's grade is independent of any other students, and there is no pre-set distribution of grades for assessment.

OBTL, characterised as Constructive Alignment, enables students to become active learners who would not only apply what they have learned into practice but also reflect on that application for continuing improvement. To this end, our University promotes authentic assessment to help students contextualise their learning and to see how real-life conditions or situations affect their theorectical knowledge. By actively engaging in their chosen academic programmes and participating in meaningful co-curricular and extra-curricular activities, it is envisaged that our students will become whole persons who can contribute to and lead our society.

# Standards of Conduct
## Principle
The University is a community pursuing academic excellence, personal wellness and spiritual maturity. All members shall maintain high honesty and conduct standards. Admitted students must abide by the University’s social, moral and academic regulations throughout study. The University upholds academic freedom, individual rights and open discussion; students shall conduct themselves with dignity and fully understand official publications.

## 1. Core Standards of Conduct
Students must comply with basic norms aligned with the University’s educational purposes and principles:
1. Respect the legitimate rights of others at all times
2. Protect personal and University safety and property
3. Uphold personal integrity and absolute honesty
4. Maintain basic civility and avoid harmful conduct to others
5. Respect all University staff and maintain friendly peer relations
6. Strictly observe all official University rules and regulations

### Typical Misconduct (Non-exhaustive List)
Violations leading to disciplinary action include: academic cheating/plagiarism; disrupting teaching/learning (talking, eating, phone use in class); drunken disorderly behavior; on-campus smoking; illegal drug possession/use; on-campus gambling; theft/damage of personal/University property; unauthorized occupation/damage of campus facilities; document forgery/falsification; indecent behavior; assault/harassment/defamation against staff/students/visitors; refusing ID verification when suspected of rule violations; violating facility usage rules; breaking local laws; hate speech/abusive/threatening language.

## 2. Sanctions for Violations
Offenders will be reviewed by designated officials/panels, with penalties based on severity:
Censure, Written Reprimand, Written Warning, Restitution, Temporary Facility Usage Ban, Mandatory Campus Voluntary Service, Temporary Ban from Student Organization/Committee Positions, Exam Suspension, Academic Probation, Study Suspension, Permanent Dismissal, Other Supplementary Sanctions

## 3. No Smoking Campus Regulation
All indoor and outdoor campus areas are strictly non-smoking zones. Smoking (including e-cigarettes and new tobacco products) is prohibited. Maximum fine for violations: HK$5,000

# Student Life
Core Units: Student Induction and Guidance, Co-curricular and Extracurricular Activities for Whole Person Education, The Career Centre (CC), Campus Life & Amenities Unit (CLA), Counselling and Development Centre (CDC), Leadership Qualities Centre (LQC), Student Accommodation Section (ACCM)

## Student Induction and Guidance
### New Student Induction
Held annually in August to help new students understand holistic development and adapt to university life. The University Life Orientation Workshop (Personal Transformation) introduces whole person development concepts and campus resources to assist life planning. Senior peer mentors support new students' adaptation. Customised training and programmes are provided for non-local students to adapt to life in Hong Kong smoothly.

### Guidance
Each new student is assigned mentors for academic and personal development guidance. Students must independently check the University Student Handbook for basic regulations, procedures and programme requirements. Final-year students shall proactively consult departments on academic progress and graduation requirements.

### Support from Unit for Students with Special Educational Needs (SEN)
The University provides equal education opportunities for SEN students. Eligible students first register documented SEN information for professional needs assessment. The unit coordinates with departments to offer tailored accommodation, facilities and learning services. Students can apply for disability grants for academic and personal development support.

## Co-curricular and Extracurricular Activities for Whole Person Education
Complementary activities are designed to help students achieve the University's Graduate Attributes and enrich learning experience alongside academic programmes.

### Creative Arts Activities
- Music: Multiple credit-bearing performing groups (orchestras, choirs, ensembles) open to all students regardless of major.
- Visual Arts: On-campus exhibitions, artist talks, guided tours, international art festival visits and community art activities; general education and professional studio courses for all students.
- Film: Masterclasses, special screenings and guest lectures by renowned industry professionals to enhance creativity and career preparation.

### Sports Programmes
Member of the University Sports Federation of Hong Kong; offers intercollegiate competitions for 18 sports for men's and women's teams, intramural sports events and Health Lifestyle general education courses.

### Student Organisations
Voluntary membership open to all students (some with membership fees). Registered/recognised bodies include: Students' Union (undergraduates), Postgraduate Association, over 30 academic societies, around 40 interest clubs, 4 Hall Council

### Student Organisations
Voluntary membership open to all students (some with membership fees). Registered/recognised bodies include: Students' Union (undergraduates), Postgraduate Association, over 30 academic societies, around 40 interest clubs, 4 Hall Councils and the International Association (supports international students' integration).

## The Career Centre (CC)
A student-centric hub providing comprehensive resources for career planning, skill improvement and employment matching.

### Core Services
1. Career Planning and Advising: One-on-one personalised consultation for interest assessment, career exploration and development planning.
2. Career Education Programmes: Career recruitment talks, CV writing workshops and interview skill training.
3. Career & Recruitment Events: Physical/online career talks, industry sharing sessions and annual on-campus Career Fair with employer booths.
4. Networking Events: Themed professional networking to connect students with employers and alumni.
5. Exploration Trips: Winter break trips to GBA, Southeast Asia and overseas cities for global workplace experience.
6. Skills Empowerment Scheme: Workshops on business etiquette, AI/GPT, Python, Power BI and ESG; online e-learning resources.
7. Entrepreneurial Support: Training, seed funding and incubation services for startup projects.
8. SEN Exclusive Career Support: Personalised counselling, matched internships and startup competition platforms.

### Internship Opportunities
Local/overseas internships, Metropolitan Attachment Programme (MAP), Global Attachment Opportunities (GAO), GBA targeted internships and STEM specialised internship schemes.

### Job Search Platform: BUhub
AI-enabled portal for internships, graduate jobs and on-campus roles; supports AI CV review, advanced job filtering and personalised matching.

### Postgraduate Exclusive Services
Self-exploration workshops, industry expert consultations and tailored career training for postgraduates. Annual Graduate Employment Survey reports are available for reference.

## Campus Life & Amenities Unit (CLA)
Enriches campus life via facility management, funding support, student governance participation and activity assistance.
1. Support for student bodies: Administrative guidance, training, financial funding for registered student organisations.
2. Amenities management: Operates Learning Commons and various collaborative campus facilities.
3. University Governance Participation: Administers student representative elections for Senate and university committees; organises student consultations with management.

## Counselling and Development Centre

2. Amenities management: Operates Learning Commons and various collaborative campus facilities.
3. University Governance Participation: Administers student representative elections for Senate and university committees; organises student consultations with management.

## Counselling and Development Centre (CDC)
Professional psychological team supports students' mental health, personal growth and potential development.

### Core Services
1. Counselling & Assessment: Individual psychotherapy, psychological tests, Whole Person Development Inventory (WPDI) and online self-assessment tests with instant feedback.
2. Psychological Development: Self-growth workshops, Mental Health First Aid courses and online e-learning modules.
3. New Student Support: Transition guidance webpage, Peer Mentor Programme, orientation workshops and whole person development mentoring; Host Family Programme for non-local students.
4. Mainland Student Services: Induction activities, Cantonese courses, individual mentoring and cultural enrichment events for adaptation to Hong Kong life.

## Leadership Qualities Centre (LQC)
Cultivates global citizenship, leadership and community service awareness via diversified non-formal learning.

### Core Programmes
1. Co-curricular Learning (CCL): Seminars, workshops and experiential activities as part of U-Life Programme.
2. Adventure Education: Outdoor challenge activities to build resilience, teamwork and courage.
3. Cultural Literacy (LIVE): Art experience platforms and ambassador training for aesthetic cultivation.
4. Leadership Development: Tailored training; House of Scholars for outstanding academic students; youth leadership mentoring schemes.
5. Global Vision: International conference nominations, Backpacker Learning and overseas Serve-cation volunteer trips.
6. Community Engagement: Skilled volunteer training, long-term NGO internships and social issue funding grants.
7. Student Partnership: Co-develop activities with students; cooperate with external organisations for peer leader training.

## Student Accommodation Section (ACCM)
Manages three types of on-campus residential facilities integrating living, learning and communication.

### Village CARE (Jockey Club Campus of Creativity)
4 residential blocks with 1,726 places; mixed RPG, local/non-local undergraduates and exchange students; 4 themed living-learning colleges with credit-bearing courses, High Table Dinners and community services; equipped with single/2-person rooms, apartments, multi-purpose halls and rooftop activity terraces.

### Undergraduate Halls

students; 4 themed living-learning colleges with credit-bearing courses, High Table Dinners and community services; equipped with single/2-person rooms, apartments, multi-purpose halls and rooftop activity terraces.

### Undergraduate Halls
4 halls named after four prominent Chinese figures with 1,770 residential places; air-conditioned rooms with LAN/Wi-Fi; public facilities include canteen, fitness rooms, study rooms and kitchens; residential education programmes guided by Resident Masters for all-round development.

### Dr Ng Tor Tai International House
Accommodates visiting scholars and full-time non-local undergraduate/postgraduate students; air-conditioned rooms with private bathrooms and basic appliances; provides regular cleaning, security patrols, shared laundry and dining areas for daily communication.

Payment Rules:
Tuition fees paid in 2 equal instalments (except trimester-based programmes), before 1st & 2nd semester start. Non-refundable after payment. Thesis-submitted RPg students pay fees until graduation status confirmed.

University Life (U-Life)
Undergraduate students are required to complete the non-credit bearing University Life programme. Workshops and activities in U-Life aim to enhance students’ capabilities to face challenges and find solutions that come from academic or work environment. After completing this programme, students should be able to:
1. Acquire the skills and knowledge that help them adapt to the new life at the University;
2. Examine issues of academic integrity and global citizenship with an awareness of their communities;
3. Develop leadership skills, communication skills and team work spirit that can be applied to their daily life.

Requirements
University Life Orientation Workshops (UOW)
1. UOW 1: Personal Transformation Workshop (by Office of Student Affairs)
This workshop presents the concept of whole person development and utilises students’ profiles from the Whole Person Development Inventory (WPDI) to enhance the awareness of their current state of development. The workshop also introduces a wide range of university resources for supporting students’ whole person development and facilitates better planning for their university life.

2. UOW 2: Insight into HKBU’s 7 Graduate Attributes (by Centre for Holistic Teaching and Learning)
In this workshop, students will use their mobile devices to go through a learning path, known as the Trail of Integrity and Ethics—General (TIE-General). This major endeavour of the New Student Orientation forewarns new students of common misunderstandings that may lead to academic misconduct. Using augmented reality and mobile technologies, TIEs are designed to engage students and motivate them to learn about academic integrity and ethics (AIE), and internalise key concepts to apply in everyday settings. Furthermore, TIE-General acts as a starting trail and students can learn discipline-specific concepts of AIE via other TIEs in subsequent years.

3. UOW 3: Get Ready for University Research (by Library)
New university students may be unaware of the expectations that their professors have in terms of the sources they should use in their assignments. This workshop begins by impressing upon students that success in their university studies will depend on their ability to locate and access reliable sources of scholarly information. Librarians are introduced as professionals that can help them develop their information literacy abilities. Students are also provided with an overview of the key services and facilities of HKBU Library to ensure that they are aware of these vital resources.

help them develop their information literacy abilities. Students are also provided with an overview of the key services and facilities of HKBU Library to ensure that they are aware of these vital resources.

Co-curricular Learning (CCL)
Co-curricular Learning (CCL) take the form of seminars, workshops, campus events, and experiential learning activities which all aim to foster students’ whole person development.
- Students are required to complete at least 8 CCL items before the end of the fourth semester of study.
- Students directly admitted to Year 2 or 3 have to complete the requirement by the end of the third semester of study, while the exemption of 4 CCL items applies to students directly admitted to Year 3.

Academic Integrity Online Tutorial (AIOT)
Academic Integrity Online Tutorial (AIOT) is an introduction to promoting academic integrity by increasing students’ awareness of the issues involved. Using pertinent cases as examples, each of the six modules in AIOT provides information and strategies that can be applied to avoid committing academic misconduct unintentionally.
- Students are required to complete AIOT before the end of the second semester of study.

Completion Assessment & Consequences
1. Students will be given an “S” grade for satisfactory completion of the programme or a “U” grade for unsatisfactory performance.
2. A student who is unable to complete the programme by the deadlines specified in the BUniPort will be barred from accessing course grades immediately and will not be allowed to enrol on courses in the following semester until his/her deficiency has been made up.
